text: A baggage cart idles on the floodlit ramp, then cuts out, and the noise drops to nothing.
Birdy stands at the foot of the airstair in his hi-vis vest, looking up at the dark open door of the parked Q400.
He glances back across the empty ramp, then climbs the airstair one slow step at a time.
He ducks through the door and stops in the black galley, letting his eyes adjust.
He steps up to the flight deck, rests a hand on the back of the left seat, and stays there a moment.
He lowers himself into the captain's seat and sits very still, hands in his lap.
Through the windscreen the runway lights run off into the dark.
He sets his feet on the rudder pedals and wraps his right hand around the yoke.
His left hand finds the power levers and rests there.
Before start checklist complete.
He thumbs an imaginary switch on the overhead and his lips move without sound.
His hand slides the power levers forward a few inches and holds them steady.
Power set.
He fixes his eyes down the dark runway and eases the yoke.
Rotate.
He draws the yoke back into his stomach, slow and even, and his feet come off the pedals.
Positive rate.
His left hand leaves the levers, reaches down to the gear handle, and lifts it.
Gear up.
He settles the yoke into a gentle bank and his shoulders turn with it.
His thumb rolls a trim wheel, easing pressure off the column.
Flaps up.
He holds the bank, eyes ahead, breathing slow, the runway lights wheeling under the nose.
A flashlight beam swings across the windscreen from the galley.
Hey.
Birdy's hands drop off the yoke into his lap.
Off the controls. Out of the seat.
Birdy turns, blinking into the light.
Sorry. Sorry, Captain.
He fumbles the seat belt loose and starts to lever himself up.
Left my good seat warming for you.
Ward stands square in the galley, headset in one hand, and does not move aside.
I forgot my headset. You can't be up here.
No. You're right.
Birdy climbs out of the seat and squeezes past Ward toward the door, head down.
Door's logged when you go.
Going.
Birdy steps out onto the airstair and works his way down to the ramp.
He stands on the floodlit concrete in his vest, the dark plane rising behind him.
A handheld radio on his belt crackles.
Birdy, you clear of the aircraft?
He lifts the radio without looking back at the plane.
Yeah. Clear.
